summary: The CNS consists of the brain and spinal cord, while the PNS consists of cranial and spinal nerves.
The CNS consists of cranial nerves and the brain, while the PNS includes only the spinal cord.
The CNS and PNS are anatomically the same but functionally different.
The sympathetic division arises from T1-L2 (thoracolumbar outflow) and mediates fight-or-flight responses.
The sympathetic division arises from craniosacral outflow and mediates rest-and-digest responses.
The sympathetic division arises from C1-C8 and only controls heart rate.
Oligodendrocytes myelinate axons in the CNS, while Schwann cells myelinate axons in the PNS.
Schwann cells myelinate axons in the CNS, while oligodendrocytes myelinate in the PNS.
Astrocytes are responsible for myelination in both the CNS and PNS.
A spinal nerve is formed by the union of a dorsal root (sensory) and a ventral root (motor).
A spinal nerve is formed by the union of two dorsal roots (one sensory, one motor).
A spinal nerve is formed from a single root that carries both sensory and motor fibres.
An upper motor neuron lesion causes spastic paralysis with increased reflexes, while a lower motor neuron lesion causes flaccid paralysis with decreased reflexes.
An upper motor neuron lesion causes flaccid paralysis, while a lower motor neuron lesion causes spastic paralysis.
Both upper and lower motor neuron lesions cause flaccid paralysis.
The parasympathetic division has a craniosacral outflow (CN III, VII, IX, X and S2-S4).
The parasympathetic division has a thoracolumbar outflow from T1-L2.
The parasympathetic division arises only from the vagus nerve (CN X).

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the organisation of the nervous system.
Good effort! Review the areas you missed.
